topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of aged paper and strong coffee hung heavy in the air of the study. I was looking for the remote, but instead, my gaze snagged on the open laptop resting on the mahogany desk. The screen displayed a meticulously formatted manuscript. My breath hitched. It was a story. And the protagonist, the central, agonizingly detailed character, was undeniably me. My name, my mannerisms, the way I habitually chewed on the end of my pen—it was all there, rendered in prose. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling at my ribs. I slowly reached out, my fingers trembling slightly, and hovered over the cursor. The silence in the room seemed to amplify the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

"Darling, are you alright?" I asked, my voice pitched a little too high as I watched him. He was engrossed, leaning back in his chair, a satisfied, almost proprietary smile playing on his lips. He gestured toward the screen with a flourish. "Just making some progress on Chapter Seven. You know, the internal conflict around the late autumn rain." My hands felt suddenly clammy. I took a deliberate step closer, my boots making soft sounds on the thick rug. When I finally looked at the words—my vulnerabilities, my secret anxieties laid bare on the page—a strange, hollow feeling settled in my chest. I cleared my throat, the sound scratchy. "That's... quite detailed."

[story 3]

I found the evidence tucked beneath a stack of reference books: a thick, leather-bound journal filled with intricate descriptions of my daily routines, my fleeting thoughts, the precise shade of blue I preferred on a Tuesday morning. My heart began to beat a rapid, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I picked up the journal, the paper surprisingly fragile beneath my fingertips. Every entry felt like an invasion, a mapping of my private landscape by someone else. I flipped a page, and there, written in a looping, elegant hand, was a description of my hesitation before answering a difficult phone call. A wave of profound stillness washed over me, a paralyzing sense of exposure. I dropped the book, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet apartment, and walked toward the door, needing air that didn't feel quite right.
